proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions are specific and consistent: the game explicitly calls itself a 'First-Person-Puzzle-Metroidvania' with an interconnected world that opens up through newly found upgrades and abilities, plus heavy emphasis on exploration, secret areas, environmental puzzle solving, and progression-gated traversal. The text repeatedly frames combat as a relatively small, sometimes puzzle-heavy component, which supports metroidvania over an action-led archetype. Detective cases and mystery-solving are notable topical layers, but the core gameplay identity is still ability-gated exploration in a handcrafted interconnected world.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Nearly identical gameplay identity: first-person ability-gated exploration, puzzle-heavy progression, dense secrets, and even less combat than the anchor.
Core template match for the anchor: first-person puzzle-metroidvania, versatile upgrades, interconnected world design, secret hunting, and minimal hand-holding.
Same series formula and priorities—exploration, puzzles, traversal abilities, and reduced combat—though it is a smaller campaign rather than a full-scale entry.
Excellent first-person metroidvania benchmark with interconnected progression and ability gating, but it is more combat-led and less sandbox-puzzly than the anchor.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Marathon as a 'PvPvE survival extraction FPS' and a 'PvP extraction shooter.' The core loop is clearly stated: infiltrate/scavenge zones, fight rival players and hostile AI, loot valuables, exfil, then use stolen loot and season-long unlocks to improve future runs. Supporting details include solo or crew-based runs, in-progress lobby insertion, finite supplies, ranked play, moddable weapons, build crafting, vault space expansion, and persistent seasonal progression. This strongly matches extraction_shooter over generic FPS labels or arena/hero shooter near-misses.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The benchmark extraction FPS: first-person raids, PvPvE threats, loadout prep, loot loss on death, exfil-driven progression, and heavy tension per run. More milsim/hardcore than Marathon, but genre-essential.

Very close loop match with solo/duo/trio PvPvE runs, contested objectives, hostile AI, rival squads, and extraction under pressure. Weapon handling and pacing differ, but the session structure is highly aligned.
A particularly apt sci-fi comp: scavenging runs, faction contracts, hostile creatures plus players, evac extraction, and gear/progression upgrades map closely to Marathon's structure.

Strong extraction overlap with tactical looting, PvEvP firefights, squad deployment, contracts, and persistent progress. It leans more open-world and realism-first than Marathon's likely faster, cleaner FPS feel.
Clearly shares the loot-shoot-extract session loop and persistent stash/shelter progression, but it is less of a direct first-person PvPvE extraction benchmark and broader in mode identity.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and concretely describe ARC Raiders as a third-person multiplayer extraction game: players enter distinct topside maps, scavenge loot, survive hostile machines and rival Raiders, then return to the underground hub/workshop to sell loot, craft, repair, upgrade stations, learn blueprints, level skills, and take trader quests. The text explicitly supports solo or up-to-3-player play, PvPvE pressure, repeated incursions with changing map conditions, and progression through gear, crafting, quests, and a branching skill tree. That combination strongly supports extraction_shooter as the core gameplay identity, with loot_shooter and tactical_shooter as secondary affinities rather than primary genre fits.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on the core raid-extract loop: PvPvE drops, hostile wildlife plus players, contracts, loot retention, and gear progression between runs. First-person perspective and a more straightforward prospecting structure keep it below a true same-fit match.

Strong fit through third-person sci-fi PvPvE extraction, underground-to-surface structure, resource scavenging, and persistent progression. It is a bit less aligned because mech/vehicle-centered play and AI-partner systems change the moment-to-moment combat identity.

A foundational extraction comp with raids, stash management, traders, quests, and severe loot-loss stakes. It sits lower because its first-person hardcore mil-sim/RPG structure is much more punishing and simulation-heavy than ARC Raiders.

Third-person extraction structure, loot runs, extraction tension, and shelter upgrading all map well. It is looser because it is more PvP-centric, less defined by PvPvE enemy ecology, and less built around squad-based sci-fi tactical encounters.

Clearly adjacent as a PvPvE extraction shooter with squad tension, AI threats, and sound/stealth management. It is less similar in objective structure and feel: boss-bounty hunts, old-weapon gunplay, and permadeath-style stakes differ meaningfully from ARC Raiders' broader scavenging-and-progression loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a classic Call of Duty package centered on first-person gunplay: a co-op/solo campaign with covert military missions, competitive multiplayer with 6v6 and 20v20 maps, and zombies as an additional mode. The copy emphasizes near-future weaponry, mastering an arsenal, Omnimovement, multiplayer map counts, and a signature campaign/multiplayer structure, which strongly supports military_fps over horror-led archetypes. Zombies and psychological-war themes are present, but they read as one component of a broader shooter package rather than the core genre identity of the whole title.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Immediate predecessor with the same campaign/multiplayer/round-based Zombies package and very similar Black Ops gunplay, pacing, and player expectations.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored Steam description explicitly says 'Read a Romantic Comedy' and 'Standard Visual Novel Mechanics,' describing text reading, skipping previously read text, and hiding the text window to view artwork. The content is centered on an authored romance story with four heroines, no combat, and single-player progression through narrative scenes, which strongly matches the visual_novel archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct Making*Lovers counterpart and the clearest comparison point: same romance-VN format, heroine routes, comedic tone, and dating-focused progression.
Very close fit as a contemporary romantic-comedy visual novel with heroine selection, interactive route progression, and a pure-love focus.
Another light multi-heroine romance VN with the same reading-driven structure and character-route emphasis; slightly less direct than Fureraba but still highly aligned.
Still a solid romance VN comp with route-based heroine stories and authored progression, though its elite-academy setup makes it a touch less everyday-dating aligned than the others.

The title suggests DLC for PowerWash Simulator 2, but no usable gameplay description, mechanics summary, perspective, session structure, or parent-game evidence is provided in the stored DB. The only stored genre/theme signal is a broad 'simulation' label from OpenCritic, which is too generic to map confidently to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct parent game match: identical pressure-washing core loop, first-person cleaning, job-based progression, and overall audience expectation.
Series foundation with the same soothing wash-to-complete structure, equipment-based progression, and low-pressure job flow, just not the anchor's actual base game.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored evidence clearly describes a standalone single-player puzzle game built around dual-layer nonogram and jigsaw challenges across 250+ puzzles, with calming presentation and difficulty ramping. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a general nonogram/jigsaw puzzle category, and the game does not materially fit hidden_object_puzzle, word_puzzle_strategy, or any other listed archetype. Rejected due to taxonomy coverage mismatch rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on the primary nonogram logic loop, high puzzle count, accessibility features, and steady solo progression; lacks the anchor’s jigsaw reconstruction layer.
Another substantial nonogram-centric puzzler with cozy pacing, lots of content, and gentle progression, though its identity is still only one half of the anchor’s hybrid structure.
Strong nonogram fit with handcrafted puzzle progression, relaxing presentation, and brain-training appeal, but it lacks the anchor’s jigsaw/art-restoration second phase.
Good match for the calm artistic jigsaw assembly side and escalating complexity, but it omits the nonogram deduction that defines much of the anchor’s core loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Minos as a maze-building roguelite centered on designing and redesigning labyrinth layouts, placing walls/gates/corridors, linking traps through pressure-plate chain reactions, and defending against invading adventurers across repeatable runs. The clearest gameplay identity is systemic construction and trap engineering rather than direct action combat, which aligns best with engineering_sandbox_sim. Roguelite run structure, procedural variation ('Every run brings new enemies, new traps'), and single-player premium replayability are all directly supported. Some presentation details like camera/perspective are not stated, so those fields are left blank.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on building lethal maze-like spaces with modular traps and routing intruders, though its PvP raiding/FPS play and asynchronous multiplayer focus make it less roguelite and less purely defensive than Minos.
Strong reverse-dungeon-defender match with roguelite structure, trap prep, and stopping hero incursions, but it is more management-heavy and turn-based than Minos's maze-engineering sandbox.

Shares evil-dungeon construction, trap placement, and defending against heroes, but its RTS/god-game economy, minion management, and broader strategy scope pull it away from Minos's run-based maze-building identity.

A sensible trap-defense comp because of chained hazards and wave stopping, but it is primarily direct action/tower defense with fixed lanes and active combat rather than player-authored labyrinth building.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator copy consistently describe a third-person action-RPG structure: exploring the area around Zoz, helping townspeople through quests, real-time slash-and-dodge combat with a machete, companions with unique combat skills, cooking for buffs, bond-building with residents, and magical weapon/tool enhancement. The strongest fit is a fantasy action RPG with quest exploration and social/progression layers. 'Open world' is not stated outright, so confidence is moderate rather than maximal, but the overall gameplay identity aligns best with open_world_fantasy_action_rpg over alternatives like loot_action_rpg or monster_hunter_style_action_rpg, which lack strong loot-chase or boss-hunt evidence here.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong overlap in action-RPG adventuring, helping townsfolk, mystery-driven fantasy story, cooking/crafting support systems, and ally-based progression, though Harvestella is more farming/life-sim structured and broader in scope.

Good match on small-town helping loop, gathering materials in the field, relationship-building with residents, and light action gameplay, but its chemist/diagnosis sim focus is less combat-centric and less party-driven than Town of Zoz.

Shares real-time combat, companions, town relationships, cooking, and community tasks, but Rune Factory 5 is much more dominated by farming, routine life-sim systems, and broader sandbox daily structure.

Comparable blend of combat, dungeon exploration, cooking, town bonds, and helping residents, though it is even more of a farming/social-life RPG than the anchor and less centered on the specific hunt-cook-bond loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a short, low-stress single-player fishing-focused experience with handcrafted art, river drifting, landmark observation, and decisions that influence the outcome. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a fitting fishing-sim or contemplative narrative-sim bucket, and the evidence does not strongly support mapping it to an existing archetype such as life_sim or visual_novel without overreaching. The title appears to center on relaxed fishing and light branching narrative rather than the defining gameplay identities represented in the taxonomy.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on short, cozy, low-pressure fishing as the main activity, with a gentle pace and light narrative framing. The garden-building sandbox makes it broader and less purely contemplative than the anchor.
Fishing remains central and it shares a relaxed, story-led structure with character interactions. It is notably more quest-, relationship-, and upgrade-driven, so it is less minimal than the anchor.

A clear fishing-led narrative game with emotional storytelling and relaxed play, but cooking, shopkeeping, town restoration, upgrades, and optional co-op make it a fuller slice-of-life RPG loop than the anchor.

Strong fishing-and-discovery overlap plus narrative mystery, but the boat upgrades, economy, navigation risk, and tension-heavy survival pressure create a substantially different challenge model and tone of play.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Prop Sumo as a 'party fighting game' centered on PvP matches where players brawl with melee moves, chain light and heavy attacks, use items, and compete for score across arena maps. The Steam detailed description adds strong fighting-game signals: 1v1 and free-for-all match formats, a Training Dojo to master moves and combos, unlockable ultimates, and round-based progression. This supports a primary identity of traditional_fighter, with party_game as a secondary flavor due to chaotic multiplayer arenas, items, and friend-focused free-for-all play.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on chaotic 3D melee party brawling, hazardous arenas, knockout-focused rounds, and local/online PvP. Less combo-structured than Prop Sumo, but the session feel is highly aligned.

Excellent fit for party-first arena scuffles with physics, items, environmental interaction, and casual-competitive multiplayer. Slightly broader minigame/physics-party identity than Prop Sumo's more fighter-like brawling.

Short-round multiplayer combat, power-ups, hazards, simple controls, and party pacing line up well. Main gap is its boomerang-centric top-down/ranged design rather than Prop Sumo's 3D melee brawler structure.

Strong benchmark for free-for-all fighting with items, stage hazards, custom rules, and versus mastery. However, it is a precise 2D platform fighter with ring-out design, noticeably different from Prop Sumo's 3D party-brawl feel.
Matches on training, versus depth, item/gadget spawns, and support for 1v1 and larger casual modes. Still a 2D platform fighter first, so the movement/combat model is a step away from the anchor.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Beyond Words as a word-based strategic puzzle roguelite: 'fusion of roguelike strategy and word-crafting,' with runs shaped by letter placement, modifiers, stickers, booster/power cards, upgrades, synergies, seeded runs, time attacks, and boss challenges. The gameplay loop is tactical board optimization and build construction across repeated runs, with no combat signals. Strong fit for word_puzzle_strategy rather than action, simulation, or hidden-object categories.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent match on run-based progression, score chasing, modifier stacking, boss/challenge structure, seeded runs, and synergy-heavy optimization; weaker on the actual verb since it is a card/poker deckbuilder rather than a word puzzle.

Strong word-construction overlap plus upgrades and endless/challenge play, but the core loop is turn-based word combat/RPG encounters rather than tactical board planning across roguelite-style puzzle runs.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Incantation as a first-person horror mystery game centered on exploring an eerie cult village, searching for clues, hiding from danger, and uncovering the truth behind a missing daughter. The repeated emphasis on fear, madness, mystery-solving, and stealth-based survival supports psychological_horror as the best fit, with stealth_action_adventure as a secondary influence. Evidence for combat-driven survival-horror is weak, so the classification stays with horror exploration and stealth rather than action combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: first-person vulnerable horror with exploration, puzzles, narrative mystery, stealthy evasion, and a strong maternal/family survival thread.
Strong overlap in first-person horror, stealth-focused avoidance, light puzzles, and missing-loved-one mystery, with little emphasis on fighting back.
Good match on first-person psychological horror, investigation, and story-led exploration, though its guided forest search and companion-tool structure make it somewhat distinct.
Shares first-person horror, cult threat, mystery, and stealth avoidance, but its sound-driven enemy system and more explicit survival-horror framing are more specialized.
Strong thematic and perspective overlap in story-heavy first-person psychological horror and ritualized family tragedy, but it is more atmospheric exploration than stealth-survival play.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently describes a single-player, third-person, narrative-driven 3D adventure centered on traversal and physics-inspired puzzle solving in thematic worlds, with dialogue and an emotional grief/legacy storyline. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a clear home for a non-combat narrative puzzle-adventure of this type. The evidence is strong for gameplay identity, but it does not strongly support any allowed primary archetype without forcing a misleading fit such as action-adventure or hidden-object puzzle.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on third-person exploration, environmental puzzle-solving, traversal, and symbolic emotional journey, with minimal combat emphasis.
Strong fit on grief-centered narrative progression and world-manipulation puzzles, though it is more platforming-led and somewhat more directed in structure.
Non-combat puzzle-adventure with healing themes, stylized realm progression, and gentle exploration; slightly lighter and more compact than the anchor.
Good match on dreamlike spaces, emotional storytelling, and rule-manipulation puzzles, but it is more abstract, stage-based, and less exploratory as a 3D adventure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a multiplayer-focused Pokémon battle product centered on traditional Pokémon battle mechanics, with Ranked, Casual, and Private Battles plus Pokémon HOME compatibility. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not include a strong fit for a match-based turn-based monster battling game without the broader campaign, exploration, or creature-collection loop expected by monster_collect_rpg. Accepting it under an existing archetype would overstate unsupported elements such as adventure structure or collection gameplay.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Battle-focused Pokémon spinoff built around traditional turn-based matches, multiplayer, cups, and imported teams; the closest structural and audience match to a standalone Pokémon battle hub.
Shares modern Pokémon battle systems, team-building, and competitive play conventions, but its core loop is still a full campaign RPG with catching, exploration, and story progression.
Uses the same underlying Pokémon battle logic and trainer progression framework, but it is primarily an adventure RPG centered on catching, travel, and campaign advancement.
Current-generation Pokémon combat overlaps well, but the open-world exploration, freeform progression, and collection-heavy campaign make it farther from Champions’ battle-platform identity.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently define The Occultist as a first-person narrative thriller with explicit survival-horror framing: 'Survival horror: The Occultist has all the distinctive elements of the genre,' combined with tense exploration, supernatural threats, ominous cult imagery, and a horror-focused abandoned-island setting. The gameplay loop centers on exploring Godstone, investigating the protagonist's father's disappearance and occult rituals, using a mystic pendulum to interact with and modify the environment, and crucially avoiding direct combat: 'Alan always avoids direct combat' and progresses via 'Hide and Sneak' stealth mechanics. That strongly supports survival_horror as the primary identity, with psychological_horror and stealth_action_adventure as meaningful secondary overlaps.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match on first-person survival horror, helplessness, exploration, and run-hide-escape structure with no real combat focus.
Strong fit: first-person survival horror with stealth avoidance, cult/supernatural framing, and narrative progression with minimal offensive capability.
Excellent first-person hide-and-survive comp with sustained stalking threat and investigative survival structure, though more systemic and resource-driven than the anchor.

Good survival-horror neighbor via first-person tension, environmental problem-solving, and threat avoidance, but its sandbox/resource-management/combat-readiness pushes it more action-survival than the anchor.
Reasonable overlap in first-person supernatural horror and exploration, but it is slower, more psychological, and less clearly structured around stealth-survival pursuit than the anchor.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Samson as a single-player open-world urban action game, not a pure racer. The core loop centers on moving through open world city districts, taking consequential jobs/missions under a debt-and-time-pressure structure, engaging in close-quarters brawler combat, and using cars as traversal/combat tools during chases and collisions. Repeated signals include 'Open World Districts,' 'Brawler Combat,' 'Cars as Weapons,' climbing through alleys and rooftops, a 25+ upgrade skill tree, law-response escalation, and story-driven progress tied to Samson's sister and criminal debt. Racing is present, but the evidence frames driving as one major verb inside a broader open-world action-adventure campaign rather than the sole governing loop.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Open-world city crime campaign with strong hand-to-hand combat, driving woven into missions, police pressure, and a grounded street-level structure that closely matches Samson’s melee-and-driving identity.

Very close on third-person open-world structure, brutal melee, vehicle combat/chases, and upgrade progression; less urban and more survival/scavenging than Samson, but the gameplay mix is highly aligned.

Grounded open-world city crime game with driving, wanted-level escalation, and mission-based progression, but its combat identity leans more toward shooting and sandbox play than close-quarters brawler focus.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as western_narrative_rpg using DLC inheritance guidance. Terran Armada is explicitly described as a story DLC for Starfield, adding new locations, technologies, weapons, and enemy encounters, with no indication of a mode or genre shift. The parent game's stored descriptions strongly establish the core identity: Bethesda-style single-player sci-fi RPG with character creation, faction and side quests, dialogue-driven roleplay, open-ended planetary exploration, ship piloting/combat, loot/crafting, and outpost building across a long-form campaign. That evidence supports a quest-driven open-world western narrative RPG more strongly than a pure shooter or action-adventure classification. The DLC copy is additive and fully consistent with that parent gameplay identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct parent game of the DLC with the same core systems, progression, combat, exploration structure, and audience expectation.
Very close Bethesda sandbox RPG comp: first-person exploration, shooting, looting, questing, crafting, and player-driven build progression.

Single-player first-person sci-fi RPG with dialogue choices, companions, ranged combat, and quest progression, though smaller-scale and less sandbox-driven.
A reasonable space action RPG comparison, but its squad focus, more directed mission structure, and distinct combat model make it a looser fit than Bethesda-style RPGs.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies ChainStaff as a 'brutal action-platformer' and '2D Platformer' with a transforming spear/grappling weapon, swinging and traversal, mutating hordes, and 'bone-shaking boss fights.' The core loop is side-scrolling combat plus platforming through a handcrafted campaign. The text also supports a single-player premium campaign structure, hand-drawn surreal presentation, grotesque sci-fi/horror setting, and some authored branching via rescue-or-harvest choices leading to different endings. Evidence is strong for action_platformer and does not strongly support a different primary archetype such as metroidvania or character_action.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on 2D action-platforming, grapple-based movement/combat, linear campaign flow, and boss/set-piece pacing; slightly less weapon-transform/tool-expression than ChainStaff.
Stage-based action-platformer with hook-assisted traversal, melee pressure, and boss-heavy pacing that maps well to ChainStaff's combat-platform structure.
Brutal linear 2D action-platforming with grotesque enemies, upgrades, and major boss emphasis; a bit more shooter/run-and-gun oriented than ChainStaff's traversal-tool combat identity.
Clear overlap in gory side-scrolling action-platforming, but it leans harder into combo-centric character action and style play than ChainStaff's grapple/spear-driven loop.
Shares brutal combat, upgrades, and memorable bosses, but its non-linear metroidvania exploration and backtracking make it a looser gameplay match.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify a single-player action RPG with randomized worlds, roguelike run structure, fast dodge-heavy combat, bosses, weapons/abilities/artifacts, per-run upgrades, and permanent meta progression. That strongly supports an action-RPG identity with replayable build variation. The main ambiguity is taxonomy fit: the game reads like a single-player action roguelite, but that exact archetype is not available in the allowed list, so loot_action_rpg is the closest supported primary archetype, with character_action as secondary due to fast aggressive dodge-based combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent gameplay match: fast top-down roguelite combat, dash/dodge focus, randomized runs, relics, elemental spell builds, and strong ability-combo identity closely mirror Eko's run-based elemental action.

A core benchmark for run-based action roguelites: aggressive real-time combat, repeated escape attempts, strong build shaping, bosses, and persistent progression align very closely even if Hades is more narrative-driven.

Very close on brisk top-down hack-and-slash, dodge timing, relic-driven builds, weapons/skills, bosses, and repeat runs; slightly less direct because of heavier co-op emphasis and more room-combat focus.

Good hybrid comp for action RPG plus roguelite progression, with procedural dungeons and persistent growth, but it is more story-led and character/family-structured, with a somewhat different run and progression feel.

Understandable fit through top-down roguelike action, repeat runs, hero builds, and exploration, but its hero-based design and stronger co-op/open-map structure make it a looser match than the tighter action-roguelite comps above.

title: Machine Child

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Machine Child as a non-combat girl-raising/nurturing simulation. The gameplay loop centers on guiding a daughter through life in a town via 700+ story events, choosing activities, outfits, and developmental paths, building affection, and reaching 22 different endings. That strongly fits life_sim with branching, relationship-driven progression and replayable single-player outcomes. Visual-novel adjacency is present through authored event scenes and multiple endings, but the core identity is life simulation rather than pure VN.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Foundational daughter-raising sim with the closest shared loop: schedule/stat management, bonding, events, and many adulthood outcomes shaped by your guidance.
Modern parenting sim built around raising a daughter through studies, activities, relationships, and heavily branching life paths; extremely close audience and gameplay fit.

Strong overlap in weekly planning, stat checks, event gating, and branching endings, but it is more a survival/royalty-training sim than a nurturing daughter-raising life sim.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Sigma Star Saga DX as a deliberate hybrid: top-down planetary RPG-style exploration combined with side-scrolling shoot-'em-up space battles, with branching story, multiple endings, boss battles, tool-based exploration, and weapon customization via Gun Data. While the evidence is strong for the game's mixed identity, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not cleanly cover this exploration-plus-shmup structure. Candidate RPG fits in the computed taxonomy are incomplete and rely on weak or mismatched assumptions about combat structure and perspective. Because its core gameplay loop materially spans an unsupported genre combination rather than aligning strongly to a single allowed archetype, rejection is more appropriate than forcing an inaccurate classification.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best fit here: it meaningfully combines campaign progression, exploration/upgrades, and sustained shoot-'em-up combat as core play rather than a small side mode. The metroidvania structure and card-spell systems make it less direct than the anchor, but the hybrid identity is close.

Strong overlap on top-down sci-fi action-RPG feel, puzzle/tool gating, quests, bosses, and progression. It misses the anchor's distinct shmup combat layer, so it fits as a structural neighbor more than a like-for-like recommendation.

Solid overlap in exploration-led progression, mode-switching between perspectives, boss/item gating, and retro action structure. However, its platformer/action-adventure identity is farther from Sigma Star Saga DX's RPG-plus-shmup mix, making it a looser comparison than the top two.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a restaurant-management/cooking simulation loop: inherit and restore a family restaurant, take customer orders, prepare meals through tactile cooking actions, upgrade tools and ingredients, customize the shop, and earn gameplay benefits from decorations. Progression is driven by improving the restaurant and building friendship levels with town residents, with a light authored story around rivals and family legacy. Very close sister-series musou: large-map staged battles, officer-centric campaigns, mass-enemy melee combat, and similar tactical battlefield flow, despite different setting and some series-specific systems.

Clearly musou and highly compatible on 1-vs-many combat, mission structure, huge roster, and co-op, but the crossover framing and three-character team-switching make it a bit less like core Dynasty Warriors moment to moment.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Crimson Desert as a third-person open-world action-adventure/RPG set in a medieval fantasy world, with free exploration across wilderness, cities, ruins, and the Abyss. Core play includes questing, battles, challenging enemies and bosses, multiple weapons and skills, elemental enhancements, dodges/counters/grapples, and traversal via climbing, gliding, and horseback. Progression includes character growth, skill development, equipment, and rebuilding/upgrading the Greymane camp, supporting an action-RPG identity over pure action-adventure. The strongest fit is open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, with secondary overlap into open_world_action_adventure and some soulslike signals from punishing boss-focused combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit on third-person single-player fantasy action RPG structure, open-world exploration, traversal, varied combat styles, and quest-driven adventure; Pawn party design is the main divergence.

Excellent match for dangerous open-world exploration, buildcraft, mounted traversal, and boss-centric combat, but it is more purely soulslike and less narrative/camp-management oriented than the anchor.

Good fit through open-world fantasy action combat, large-monster encounters, vocation-based progression, and questing, though its older design and companion-driven structure make it less aligned than Dragon's Dogma 2.

Strong overlap in single-player fantasy action RPG combat and challenging boss fights, but it is less clearly an open-world systems-driven quest RPG and misses more of Crimson Desert's broader exploration/settlement structure.

Reasonable comp for third-person open-world fantasy questing, horseback travel, and action combat, but its core identity is more narrative-choice RPG than combat-system showcase or boss-forward action adventure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Scritchy Scratchy as a scratch-card incremental/idle-style progression game centered on buying scratch-offs, revealing symbols, unlocking auto-scratching, taking risks on higher-variance cards, and prestiging for permanent upgrades. That establishes a clear gameplay identity, but it does not map cleanly to any available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not supported as pinball, racer, sports, card battler, management tycoon, or other listed archetypes, and forcing a fit would misclassify the title. Rejecting is appropriate because the game appears to sit outside the current archetype set rather than lacking evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on core idle/incremental loop: manual actions scale into automation, numbers explode, and permanent reset progression drives long-term play, though it lacks the anchor's luck-gambling focus.
Good fit for passive income growth, automation managers, offline accumulation, and prestige resets, but it plays more like a business idle sim than a luck-driven scratch-card progression game.

Shares gambling-style variance, payout chasing, and build-synergy decision making, but its roguelite slot-machine runs and deckbuilder structure are meaningfully different from the anchor's persistent idle/prestige loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present DigDigDrill as a single-player underground mining and resource-collection game focused on digging for minerals and treasure, returning to the surface when inventory is full, and forging/customizing drills through a puzzle-like smithing/build-synergy system. The text explicitly says mining occurs 'without enemies or time limits,' which weakens any combat-driven archetype despite the 'hack-and-slash style equipment system' phrasing. The dominant loop appears to be relaxed resource gathering, build crafting, and progression through deeper floors, but the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clear non-combat mining / digging / crafting progression archetype that this cleanly fits. Accepting would require forcing the game into a combat, survival, sandbox, or management identity not strongly supported by the evidence.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: A Game About Digging A Hole
staged_neighbors.requested_title: A Game About Digging A Hole
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best match for relaxed dig-deeper, collect, sell/upgrade progression with minimal pressure. Simpler and lighter than the anchor, but very close in low-stress excavation loop.
Strong overlap in drilling for minerals, hauling resources, and buying better equipment to go deeper. Broader co-op/action framing and puzzle elements make it less aligned than the top fit.
Shares descend-mine-upgrade-town loop, but the platforming, enemies, puzzles, and metroidvania structure push it away from DigDigDrill's relaxed non-combat identity.
Another credible mining comp through digging, treasure collection, and gear upgrades, but even more shaped by combat, traversal abilities, and metroidvania gating than the anchor.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies Defending Camelot as a tower defense game explicitly compared to Plants vs. Zombies, with 160 campaign levels, unit unlocking, coin collection, power-ups, and waves of monsters prevented from entering Camelot. That is strong evidence for tower defense gameplay, but tower defense is not represented by a suitable archetype in the provided Similar Games Taxonomy V2 list. Available archetypes like rts or side_scrolling_action_strategy would misclassify the core gameplay identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The defining benchmark: lane-based wave defense, staged campaign progression, roster unlocking, and direct audience overlap. The anchor explicitly cites Plants vs. Zombies as its model.
A very close tower defense comp with map-by-map campaign structure, enemy counterplay, towers/spells, and long-form progression, though less specifically PvZ-like in lane structure.
Another strong Kingdom Rush-style match: campaign TD, upgrades, hero abilities, enemy variety, and structured levels. Slightly looser than PvZ due to stronger hero-focus and broader map design.
Clearly in the same tower defense space, but its freer movement, top-down/close camera options, and strong co-op identity make it a less exact match for the anchor’s more classic staged defense loop.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports a non-combat, cozy single-player handcrafted puzzle game focused on coloring stained glass, rule-based logic puzzles, secret optional puzzles, and a level editor. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not include a general logic/puzzle-game bucket. The system-generated near miss for hidden_object_puzzle is not well supported by the actual description, which emphasizes rule-based handcrafted puzzles rather than object-finding scenes. Because the strongest evidence indicates a puzzle identity that does not cleanly map to an allowed archetype, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate classification.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent match on solo handcrafted puzzle progression, layered rule discovery, optional secrets, and respect-for-the-player design; first-person open-world structure is a notable but acceptable difference.

Very strong fit for relaxed non-combat puzzle exploration with elegant mechanics, curated content, and off-path discoveries, though its push-tree systemic puzzles differ from the anchor's art-restoration rule sets.

Shares cozy handcrafted art-puzzle appeal, simple-rules-to-complex-solutions structure, and broad solo accessibility, but its grid-filling progression is more abstract and level-pack driven than the anchor's exploratory handcrafted journey.
Strong pure-logic comparison with hand-designed puzzles, escalating challenge, and no-combat single-player play, but it is much harsher, more abstract, and less exploratory or cozy than the anchor.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a single-player detective mystery built around examining documents, photographs, and artifacts, following leads, and making deductions with no hand-holding. Source labels include Point-and-Click, and the computed evidence strongly supports cursor-driven interaction, puzzle gating, environmental puzzle solving, authored linear structure, mystery-solving goals, and no combat. That aligns best with hidden_object_puzzle in this taxonomy, despite the game emphasizing deduction/investigation more than literal hidden-object scenes.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: One of the clearest comps for deduction-first mystery design built around observing scenes, parsing evidence, and filling in conclusions through logic.

Very strong fit on document digging, cross-referencing evidence, and making independent deductions, though its genealogy/database structure is a bit more specialized than the anchor's broader case investigation.

A landmark logic-deduction mystery with strong puzzle gating and no combat, but its first-person spatial exploration and identity-solving loop differ from the anchor's document-and-leads sleuthing format.

current_live_neighbors.title: Harold Rabbit - Finder of Lost Things

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store descriptions consistently present a business-management simulator loop: rent and sell movies, organize inventory, hire staff, manage returns and fees, decorate and expand the shop, and respond to seasonal customer demand. This strongly supports management_tycoon over city_builder/colony_sim because the focus is operating and optimizing a commercial venue rather than urban planning or autonomous colony survival. Evidence also clearly supports single-player, non-combat, sandbox-style progression with base expansion and sim-oriented challenges.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match in first-person retail management: order stock, organize shelves, serve customers, handle checkout, hire staff, design layout, and expand the store through steady business growth.

Strong business-sim overlap in hands-on service, renovation, decorating, staffing, and expansion, but the gas station loop spreads into fuel, repairs, and roadside services rather than tightly focused shop inventory/rental operations.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store and aggregator copy consistently describes a train-driving simulation built around taking the controls of Thomas and other engines from an interactive first-person cab view. The game emphasizes carrying passengers, hauling freight, shunting, running timetable services, and a free-roam mode, with stories and exploration across Sodor. It is explicitly described as being powered by Train Sim World with realism/simulation technology but accessible controls, strongly supporting transport_sim over racing or adventure archetypes. Single-player only, non-combat, vehicle-control-centric, and simulation-driven structure are all directly supported by the provided text.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Essential comp: same Train Sim World train-driving foundation, first-person cab operation, route services, scenarios, and free-roam/sandbox-style play. Harder-edged and less child-focused, but gameplay identity is nearly identical.

Another extremely close match built on the same rail-sim structure: learning train operation, running routes/services, and free roam from the cab. Slightly less direct than the newest entry, but still an obvious rail candidate.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently and concretely describe a first-person survival horror campaign set in Sker Hotel where the player must stay hidden, manage breathing and noise, evade enemies that hunt by sound, solve puzzle mechanisms, explore for clues and notes, and use a limited defensive device only to stun enemies and escape. The copy repeatedly names the game 'survival horror' and emphasizes stealth, close-quarters tension, exploration, and multiple endings shaped by clues, which strongly supports survival_horror as the core gameplay identity. Stealth-action and psychological horror elements are present, but the dominant loop is survival-focused horror exploration and evasion rather than action combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct flat-screen counterpart with virtually the same first-person stealth-survival horror loop, sound-sensitive enemies, limited defensive device, clue-driven exploration, and branching endings.
Very close on VR first-person horror, hiding/evasion, environmental interaction, puzzle solving, and low-combat vulnerability, though supernatural powers make it somewhat less survival-grounded.

Strong VR survival horror fit with claustrophobic exploration, resource pressure, puzzles, and vulnerable encounters, though it is more combat/resource-driven than Maid of Sker VR's sound-stealth emphasis.
Shares first-person VR horror, exploration, and vulnerability, but its core identity is more story-driven psychological horror than stealth-survival systems play.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and corpus text consistently describe a top-down Survivor-style action roguelite built around 1-4 player co-op runs, fighting hordes of enemies, stacking upgrades each run, combining weapons and trinkets, and culminating in boss battles. The core loop is much closer to a co-op action roguelite than beat-em-up or RPG alternatives. Strong signals include explicit 'top-down roguelite action,' 'Survivor-style combat,' high replayability, solo and up-to-4-player co-op, unlockable upgrades that grow stronger every run, and diverse enemies plus epic boss battles. Fantasy kingdom/warrior framing supports high_fantasy and heroic tone.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on horde-survival action roguelite loop, boss focus, build synergies, and multiple characters, but weaker mode fit since it is primarily single-player rather than 1-4 co-op.
A core benchmark for Survivor-style runs, upgrade stacking, and overwhelming swarms; less aligned on active melee/ranged hero combat and boss-centric co-op emphasis.
Top-down 1-4 co-op roguelike action with distinct heroes, horde fights, and boss runs matches the anchor's party-based action well, though its map/objective structure is less purely Survivor-like.
Strong co-op/top-down/action-roguelite overlap with build synergies and bosses, but it plays more like a room-based hack-and-slash roguelite than a Survivor-style swarm survival game.
Good match on horde survival, hero builds, and boss encounters, but the single-player focus and more Diablo-like retro action framing make it a looser fit than the others.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions are consistent and strong: this is a single-player survivors-like/top-down shoot-'em-up action roguelike built around repeated runs, endless enemy waves, bullet-hell dodging, procedurally generated weapons, stacking upgrades, and build-driven survival. However, the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clean fit for a single-player survivors-like/top-down action roguelite shooter. Available shooter and roguelite archetypes are materially mismatched (for example, hero shooter, military FPS, tactical shooter, extraction shooter, loot shooter, or co-op action roguelite). Because the gameplay identity is clear but does not map reliably to an allowed archetype, this title should be rejected rather than force-fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Extremely close match on survivors-like arena loop, horde clearing, bullet-heavy dodging, deep build synergies, weapon crafting, unlock-based progression, and endless power scaling.
Genre-defining survivors-like comp with the same run-based survival structure, escalating swarms, upgrade drafting, and snowball progression, even if its presentation is more minimalist.
Very close on dark horde survival, repeated 30-minute runs, hero/build synergies, meta progression, and boss-inflected wave survival; slightly more Diablo-like in feel.
Strong fit through top-down arena survival, fast runs, heavy stat/buildcraft, and overwhelming enemy waves, though its discrete wave/shop cadence is a bit less like continuous survivors-style flow.

Good overlap in short-run survival roguelite structure, dense enemy pressure, dodging, and weapon-centered builds, but it leans more manual twin-stick shooting than typical auto-attack survivors-like play.
